skill.name: neuropixels-analysis
skill.description: Neuropixels analysis workflow for loading SpikeGLX, Open Ephys, and NWB recordings; preprocessing signals; checking drift; running spike sorters; computing quality metrics; curating units; and exporting results for Phy, NWB, or reports.
skill.summary: Analyze Neuropixels recordings from raw data through spike sorting, quality metrics, curation, and export.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most command, weak-crypto, C2, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from Markdown examples or neuroscience terminology. The confirmed risks are legitimate for this skill: executable Python scripts write local analysis outputs, SpikeInterface may run external spike sorters, and optional AI-assisted curation can send generated unit plots to third-party API clients. No prompt injection attempts, malicious credential exfiltration, or hidden network callbacks were found.

medium_findings.title: External Spike Sorter Execution Through SpikeInterface
medium_findings.description: The scripts invoke SpikeInterface run_sorter with a selected sorter such as Kilosort4, Kilosort3, SpykingCircus2, or Mountainsort5. This is expected scientific workflow behavior, but it can execute installed sorter toolchains or containerized dependencies on local Neuropixels data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct calls to si.run_sorter are present in executable scripts and templates. The sorter choices are constrained and domain-appropriate, so this is a legitimate elevated-risk capability rather than malicious command injection.

medium_findings.title: Local Filesystem Writes To User-Selected Output Paths
medium_findings.description: The bundled scripts create output directories and write preprocessed recordings, quality metrics, labels, summaries, plots, analyzer folders, and Phy exports. This is required for analysis, but users should run it in intended project directories because large files can be created or overwritten.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly creates folders and writes CSV, JSON, binary-folder analyzer data, plots, and export directories. These writes are bounded by user-provided output paths and do not target sensitive system locations.

medium_findings.title: Optional Third-Party AI Curation May Share Scientific Data
medium_findings.description: The AI-assisted curation guide shows Anthropic and OpenAI client usage for visual unit analysis. This is optional and user-configured, but generated plots or unit summaries may leave the local environment when an API client is used.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references document API clients and visual curation workflows, but no bundled script silently sends data by default. The privacy risk depends on whether the user enables an external API client.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified As Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are fenced Bash or Python examples in documentation, such as resource usage, install commands, and user-invoked scripts. They are not Ruby backtick execution and do not run automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are clearly inside Markdown code fences or documentation examples. They describe commands users may run manually rather than executable code paths in the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto And C2 Keyword Hits Are Neuroscience False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 detections map to domain words and symbols such as spike amplitudes, SC2 sorter names, MUA labels, thresholds, and metric abbreviations. No hashing, encryption, beaconing, or command-and-control behavior was found in the reviewed contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples are scientific analysis terms and plotting code. They do not perform cryptographic operations or network control behavior.

low_findings.title: API Key Examples Use Placeholders Rather Than Embedded Secrets
low_findings.description: The env_access findings point to documentation examples that use placeholder strings for API keys. No real secret value or process environment extraction was found at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show api_key="your-api-key" in documentation. This is a placeholder pattern, not a hardcoded credential or credential harvesting behavior.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Hits Are Local Recording Metadata Prints
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections correspond to printing recording dimensions, durations, sampling rates, GPU availability examples, and local progress information. This is expected data exploration output for electrophysiology workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed code reports properties of the loaded recording or local compute environment for analysis setup. No host inventory, credential discovery, or network reconnaissance was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sorter Invocation Can Execute Installed Toolchains
dangerous_patterns.description: SpikeInterface run_sorter dispatches to external spike sorting implementations selected by the user. Keep sorter packages and containers trusted and pinned for reproducible analysis.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code calls si.run_sorter directly with constrained sorter names. This is legitimate but can execute substantial external dependencies.

dangerous_patterns.title: Analysis Outputs Are Written To Caller-Controlled Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts save analysis artifacts to paths supplied through command-line arguments or constants. Users should review output paths before running workflows on shared systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Path creation and file writes are explicit and required for the workflow. No evidence shows hidden writes outside user-selected analysis directories.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional API Clients For Visual Curation
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation demonstrates Anthropic and OpenAI clients for visual unit assessment. Users should avoid sending sensitive or restricted datasets to external APIs without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: API usage is documented and optional, not automatic. The risk is privacy-related when users provide a client and send generated plots for analysis.

content.value_statement: Neuropixels recordings require careful preprocessing, sorting, motion checks, and unit quality review. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through reproducible analysis workflows and outputs.
actual_capabilities: Guides loading of SpikeGLX, Open Ephys, and NWB Neuropixels recordings.
Provides preprocessing steps for filtering, phase shift correction, bad channel removal, and common referencing.
Explains drift checking, motion correction, and sorter selection for Kilosort4 and CPU alternatives.
Includes scripts for preprocessing, spike sorting, metric computation, recording exploration, and Phy export.
Documents Allen, IBL, and strict curation thresholds for classifying units.
Supports AI-assisted visual review of unit waveforms when the user provides an API client or image context.
limitations: It depends on external scientific packages such as SpikeInterface, Kilosort, and sorter-specific dependencies.
Kilosort workflows may require a compatible GPU and local CUDA setup.
Automated unit labels are not a substitute for expert review in high-stakes studies.
Optional AI curation can expose generated plots or summaries to third-party API providers.

use_cases.title: Process a New Recording Session
use_cases.description: Load raw Neuropixels data, preprocess it, check drift, run sorting, and create standard result folders.

use_cases.title: Standardize Unit Quality Review
use_cases.description: Apply Allen, IBL, or strict metrics to classify good units, multi-unit activity, and noise.

use_cases.title: Prepare Manual Curation Exports
use_cases.description: Generate quality metrics, reports, and Phy exports for review by an electrophysiology expert.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single recording.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Neuropixels analysis workflow for one SpikeGLX session. Include preprocessing, drift check, sorting, metrics, and export steps.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Sorting Settings
prompt_templates.scenario: Select a sorter and key parameters.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a Neuropixels 1.0 recording with moderate drift and access to a CUDA GPU. Recommend sorter settings and preprocessing choices.

prompt_templates.title: Review Quality Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these unit quality metric summaries and suggest Allen, IBL, or strict thresholds for classifying good units.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reproducible Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reproducible multi-session Neuropixels pipeline with motion correction, sorter comparison, unit curation, Phy review, and final NWB export.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks how to analyze a new SpikeGLX Neuropixels 1.0 session.
output_examples.output: A staged workflow covering file validation, filtering, phase shift correction, bad channel detection, drift plotting, Kilosort4 sorting, metric computation, curation thresholds, and export review.

output_examples.input: A lab analyst provides unit metrics and asks which units should be kept.
output_examples.output: A concise curation summary that separates likely good units, multi-unit activity, noise, and units that need manual waveform review.

output_examples.input: A core facility needs outputs for manual Phy review.
output_examples.output: A checklist of analyzer requirements, waveform and amplitude computations, Phy export steps, and documentation items for review provenance.

best_practices: Run workflows inside a dedicated project output directory and confirm paths before starting long jobs.
Use automated metrics for first-pass curation, then manually review uncertain or important units.
Record sorter versions, thresholds, motion settings, and excluded channels for reproducibility.
anti_patterns: Do not run Kilosort workflows before checking preprocessing, probe geometry, and drift.
Do not treat AI-assisted curation as final ground truth without human review.
Do not send restricted or unpublished data to external APIs without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill run spike sorting automatically?
faq.answer: It provides scripts and guidance. Users choose when to run local commands and which sorter to use.

faq.question: Which data formats are covered?
faq.answer: The skill covers SpikeGLX, Open Ephys, and NWB workflows through SpikeInterface readers.

faq.question: Is a GPU required?
faq.answer: A GPU is recommended for Kilosort4. CPU alternatives such as SpykingCircus2 and Mountainsort5 are also documented.

faq.question: Can it perform manual curation?
faq.answer: It can export to Phy and guide manual review, but the user performs final curation decisions.

faq.question: Does AI curation send data outside my machine?
faq.answer: Only if you configure an external API client. Local Claude Code image review does not require the documented API setup.

faq.question: What security precautions should users take?
faq.answer: Use trusted sorter installations, check output paths, and avoid sending sensitive recordings or plots to external APIs without authorization.
